title: Ethiopia, Madagascar, Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD), and Centre for Coordination of Agricultural Research and Development for Southern Africa (CCARDESA) : Food Systems Resilience Program for Eastern and Southern Africa using the Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) - Chair Summary
text:
﻿Ethiopia, Madagascar, Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD), and Centre
    for Coordination of Agricultural Research and Development for Southern Africa
                                     (CCARDESA)

 Food Systems Resilience Program for Eastern and Southern Africa using the Multiphase
                            Programmatic Approach (MPA)

                                         Chair Summary*

                                           June 21, 2022

The Executive Directors approved the Food Systems Resilience Program (FSRP) for the Eastern
and Southern Africa Program using the Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) for a total
financing envelope of US$2.3 billion equivalent, including: (i) a Phase 1 of US$788.1 million
comprising US$600 million to Ethiopia, US$158.1 million to Madagascar, US$25 million to
Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD) and US$5 million to Centre for
Coordination of Agricultural Research and Development for Southern Africa (CCARDESA); and
(ii) subsequent phases for the equivalent of US$1,511.9 million to Comoros, Democratic Republic
of Congo, Lesotho, Malawi, Mozambique, South Sudan, Tanzania, Zambia, Zimbabwe, and
additional regional organizations.

Directors noted the unprecedented food and nutrition crisis in East and Southern Africa, which has
deteriorated over the years and has currently reached alarming levels. They expressed their strong
support for the ambition of the World Bank to help countries fight food insecurity in the region in
the short, medium, and long term. They welcomed the regional integration design of the operation
and the proposed support to regional organizations.

Directors noted that the program includes a component on emergency interventions and highly
appreciated the focus of the FRSP on the medium- and long-term resilience of food systems. They
stressed that this is critical for longer-term sustainability and reduction of impacts of the multiple
crises on vulnerable populations. Directors highlighted the need to ensure close coordination
between the operation and other country programs that are specifically targeted to address the
immediate food insecurity crisis.

Directors highly appreciated the innovation of the operation taking a multi-sectoral food systems
approach. They also commended the menu of options provided under the components of the
operation, which allow adaptation to country circumstances and cross-learning among the
beneficiary countries. Directors stressed the need to support productive infrastructure and tackle
food loss and waste across the value chain, noting the importance of supporting agricultural policy
reforms and addressing food production in the long term. They called for further efforts to address
gender-related challenges in a more integrated and mainstreamed manner. They also underscored
the role of the private sector and encouraged close WBG collaboration in the food security space.

Directors took note of the risks associated with the implementation of this program, particularly in
conflict-affected areas. Directors appreciated the program’s adaptive approach to addressing
implementation risks, including the use of a third-party implementation agency in conflict areas in
Ethiopia. They underscored the importance of strong results and monitoring framework and
integration of lessons learned to maximize the impact of the program given the fragile situation in
 the region. They highlighted the importance of continued cooperation with development partners.
They also encouraged regular briefings to the Board on implementation progress.

Finally, Directors encouraged cross-learning between this FRSP program and the recently
approved West Africa Food Systems Resilience and other efforts to further improve resilience at
the continental level, including by strengthening the capacity of relevant regional organizations.




_____________________________
*This Summary is not an approved record.
